architecture.summary: The paper decomposes MLP layer activations in LLMs using seminonnegative matrix factorization (SNMF) to extract interpretable features. SNMF learns sparse linear combinations of co-activated neurons that are directly mapped to their activating inputs, creating features that are both sparse and interpretable by construction. The learned features are then evaluated via causal intervention (steering) against residual-stream SAE baselines and a supervised difference-in-means baseline.

biblio.title: Constructing Interpretable Features from Compositional Neuron Groups

claims.statement: SNMF-derived features are sparse linear combinations of co-activated neurons mapped to their activating inputs, making them directly interpretable
SNMF features outperform SAEs and a strong supervised baseline (difference-in-means) on causal steering
SNMF-extracted features align with human-interpretable concepts
Specific neuron combinations are reused across semantically-related features, exposing a hierarchical structure in the MLP's activation space
SAEs often struggle in causal evaluations and lack intrinsic interpretability, as their learning is not explicitly tied to the computations of the model
Experiments conducted on Llama 3.1, Gemma 2, and GPT-2
